questions.question: Explain the difference between greedy best-first search and A* search. Why is A* preferred for finding optimal solutions?
keyPoints: Greedy uses only h(n), A* uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
Greedy doesn't consider path cost from start
A* guarantees optimal solution with admissible heuristic
Greedy can get stuck in non-optimal paths/loops
A* is optimally efficient among admissible heuristic searches

questions.question: Define admissibility and consistency of heuristic functions. Why are these properties important in A* search?
keyPoints: Admissible: never overestimates true cost (h(n) ≤ h*(n))
Consistent: h(n) ≤ c(n,n') + h(n') for all successors
Consistency ensures f(n) is non-decreasing along any path
Admissible ensures optimality with tree search
Consistency needed for optimality with graph search and to avoid re-expanding nodes

questions.question: What is the time and space complexity of A* search? Why is memory often the limiting factor?
keyPoints: Time complexity: O(b^d) exponential in solution depth
Space complexity: O(b^d) exponential, stores all generated nodes
Memory is limiting because all nodes kept in OPEN and CLOSED sets
Practical limit often determined by available memory, not CPU time
Can be mitigated using IDA*, RBFS, or weighted A*

questions.question: Explain the working of A* algorithm with the help of OPEN and CLOSED lists.
keyPoints: OPEN contains generated but not yet expanded nodes (priority queue by f(n))
CLOSED contains already expanded nodes
Start: add initial node to OPEN
Loop: remove node with lowest f(n) from OPEN
If goal, return path. Else expand, add successors to OPEN, add node to CLOSED
Graph search: skip successors already in CLOSED with lower g-cost

questions.question: Compare Manhattan distance and number of misplaced tiles as heuristics for the 8-puzzle problem. Which one is better and why?
keyPoints: Misplaced tiles: counts tiles not in correct position
Manhattan: sum of distances of tiles from correct position
Manhattan dominates misplaced tiles (Manhattan ≥ Misplaced for all states)
Dominant heuristic expands fewer nodes
Manhattan is more accurate as it considers actual moves needed

questions.question: What is a dominant heuristic? Given two admissible heuristics h1 and h2, under what condition does h2 dominate h1?
keyPoints: Heuristic h2 dominates h1 if h2(n) ≥ h1(n) for all nodes n
Both must be admissible (never overestimate)
Dominant heuristic expands fewer nodes
Leads to faster search while maintaining optimality
If h2 dominates h1, always prefer h2 for A* search

questions.question: Explain why A* with an admissible heuristic is optimally efficient. What does this mean?
keyPoints: No other algorithm can guarantee optimal solution with fewer node expansions
Given the same heuristic, A* expands minimum possible nodes
Proof by contradiction: any algorithm expanding fewer nodes must miss a node on optimal path
Optimal efficiency assumes consistent/admissible heuristic
This makes A* the best choice when optimality is required

questions.question: Illustrate the execution of A* search for the 8-puzzle problem using the initial state shown. Trace the algorithm step by step showing OPEN and CLOSED lists.
keyPoints: Define initial and goal state clearly
Show expansion order with f, g, h values
Maintain OPEN as priority queue sorted by f(n)
Update g and f values for successors
Show CLOSED list after each expansion
Continue until goal is reached
Trace should show optimal solution found

questions.question: Explain the A* algorithm with pseudocode. Discuss tree search vs graph search variants and their optimality conditions.
keyPoints: Write complete pseudocode with initialization, main loop, goal test, expansion
Tree search: doesn't track visited states, may re-expand
Graph search: uses CLOSED to avoid re-expansion
Optimality with tree search: admissibility sufficient
Optimality with graph search: consistency also required
Explain why consistency needed for graph search
Discuss memory implications of both variants

questions.question: Consider the graph below and apply both Greedy Best-First Search and A* Search to find the path from Start to Goal. Compare the results and explain why they differ.
keyPoints: Define a sample graph with node costs and heuristic values
Apply Greedy: always expand node with lowest h(n)
Apply A*: always expand node with lowest f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
Show both searches find (possibly different) paths
Greedy may not find optimal path
A* finds optimal path with admissible heuristic
Explain differences in node expansions and final path costs

questions.question: What are the limitations of A* search? Discuss memory-bounded heuristic search techniques to overcome these limitations.
keyPoints: Main limitation: exponential space complexity O(b^d)
Cannot solve problems with large search spaces
IDA*: iterative deepening with f-cost bound, O(bd) space
RBFS: recursive best-first search, uses recursion stack
SMA*: Simplified Memory-bounded A*, uses available memory
Weighted A*: trades optimality for speed with f = g + W×h
Discuss trade-offs of each approach
